system_context.business_goals: Ease the use of FL in real-world cross-silo applications, emphasize collaboration between participants, combine governance with authentication, and offer traceability of governance decisions and training processes.

architectures.name: FL-APU (Federated Learning - Application for Practical Use)
architectures.description: A scenario-based, cross-silo federated learning architecture designed for multiple organizations (e.g., energy companies) to collaboratively train and improve machine learning models without sharing raw data. It emphasizes governance, security, and traceability in inter-organizational settings.

key_components.responsibilities: Manages the negotiation process between collaborating parties and stores all governance decisions (e.g., data usage policies, model sharing rules) in an immutable governance contract.
Registers new FL participants (client organizations), performs authentication, and validates all registration requests against governance policies.

Orchestrates the core federated learning process: coordinates training rounds, aggregates model updates from clients, validates data schemas, preprocesses metadata, and evaluates global model performance.
Manages all secure communication between the FL Server and FL Clients. Handles encryption/decryption and compression for data in transit.
Deploys the global model to client infrastructure, manages local model personalization, handles inference requests, and monitors model performance on the client side.

architectures.data_flow: 1. Governance & Onboarding: A new client registers via Client Management, which authenticates them and checks against Governance Manager policies. 2. FL Training Cycle: The FL Manager initiates a round, sending the global model via the Communicator to authenticated clients. Clients train locally and send encrypted updates back through the Communicator. The FL Manager aggregates updates to create a new global model. 3. Deployment & Inference: The updated model is sent via the Communicator to the FL Client Model Deployer, which deploys it for local inference and monitoring. All governance decisions and training metadata are logged for traceability.
architectural_constraints: Designed for cross-silo scenarios with few participants (e.g., 2-10 organizations) but each with substantial computational/data resources.
Cannot use a Virtual Private Network (VPN) for interconnection.
Must use an RPC framework that supports HTTP/2-based communication (e.g., gRPC).
External FL Server cannot proactively send messages that trigger operations inside a client's private infrastructure; clients must initiate or poll for updates.
architectures.notes: The architecture is explicitly designed for controlled, secure collaboration between independent organizations. Its strength is in governance and traceability, but it is not optimized for large-scale, public-facing applications with thousands of participants (cross-device FL). Changes to the governance model or authentication protocol will have widespread impact due to the central role of the Governance Manager and Client Management components.

scenario_elicitation.elicitation_method: AI-Driven Brainstorming based on System Context

scenarios.scenario_text: The system must allow a new energy company to join the FL collaboration within 24 hours, with the process requiring only configuration of local data sources and authentication credentials, and no changes to the central FL-APU components.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Direct match. Current 'Client Management' and 'Governance Manager' can handle onboarding and authentication; 'Communicator' manages secure connections. Requires robust configuration interfaces.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Eases real-world deployment and encourages multi-company collaboration by minimizing technical and procedural barriers to entry.

scenarios.scenario_text: The system must provide an immutable audit log that records every governance decision (e.g., model selection, participant expulsion) and each client's contribution to every federated training round, accessible for compliance review for at least 7 years.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Direct match implied by architecture's emphasis on traceability. 'Governance Manager' must log decisions; 'FL Manager' and 'Communicator' must log training contributions. Requires a persistent, secure logging subsystem.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Offers traceability of governance decisions and training processes, which is critical for trust, compliance, and debugging in an inter-organizational setting.

scenarios.scenario_text: A data scientist (end-user) must be able to initiate a new federated training task for energy load forecasting by selecting a pre-configured model template, defining participating companies, and launching the task via a single API call or UI action.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Direct match. 'FL Manager' orchestrates training; 'Client Management' identifies participants; 'Governance Manager' authorizes the task. Requires a well-defined, simple interface atop these components.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Eases the use of FL in real-world applications by abstracting complexity and emphasizing collaborative task initiation.

scenarios.scenario_text: The system must support the integration of a new, custom authentication provider (e.g., company-specific OAuth server) without requiring modifications to the core FL training or communication protocols.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect match. Architecture emphasizes combining governance with authentication. This scenario tests the modularity and extensibility of the authentication mechanism within the 'Governance Manager' and 'Communicator'.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Facilitates collaboration between participants from diverse organizations, each potentially having their own security infrastructure, by allowing flexible authentication integration.

scenarios.scenario_text: The system must enable a participating company to deploy a newly aggregated global FL model to its production energy forecasting pipeline automatically, within one hour of model finalization, using its existing CI/CD tools.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Direct match for intent, but requires mature automation. 'FL Client Model Deployer' is the key component. Feasibility depends on its integration capabilities with diverse client-side systems and the reliability of the 'Communicator' for model delivery.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Tailors the architecture for practical use by closing the loop from collaborative training to real-world application, improving model quality impact.

scenarios.scenario_text: The system must allow for exploratory simulation of a training round with hypothetical data distributions from new participant types (e.g., a new renewable energy provider) to predict its impact on model convergence before formal onboarding.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect match. Not a stated core function. Would require significant extension to the 'FL Manager' for simulation and sandboxing, and likely a new component for synthetic data management. 'Governance Manager' would manage simulation permissions.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Supports improved collaboration and model quality by de-risking the addition of new, heterogeneous data sources to the federated learning process.

scenario_classification.rationale: The architecture's 'Client Management' (C-02) handles registration and authentication, while 'Governance Manager' (C-01) stores policies for validation. 'Communicator' (C-04) manages secure connections. The process aligns with the data flow for onboarding, requiring only client-side configuration and no central changes.

While the architecture emphasizes traceability, it lacks explicit logging components. 'Governance Manager' (C-01) and 'FL Manager' (C-03) must log decisions and contributions, but current descriptions don't specify persistent, immutable audit logs with 7-year retention. This requires architectural enhancements for logging, storage, and compliance.

'FL Manager' (C-03) orchestrates training, 'Client Management' (C-02) identifies participants, and 'Governance Manager' (C-01) authorizes tasks. A simple interface (API/UI) can abstract these components, allowing task initiation as described without core changes.

The architecture combines governance with authentication, but modularity for custom authentication providers isn't specified. 'Governance Manager' (C-01) and 'Communicator' (C-04) would need extensible authentication interfaces to integrate external OAuth servers without modifying core protocols.

'FL Client Model Deployer' (C-05) handles deployment, but automation within one hour using existing CI/CD tools requires robust integration capabilities and reliable model delivery via 'Communicator' (C-04). Current architecture doesn't specify these automation features, necessitating enhancements.

Exploratory simulation with hypothetical data isn't a core function. 'FL Manager' (C-03) would need simulation/sandboxing extensions, and a new component for synthetic data management. 'Governance Manager' (C-01) would manage permissions, but this represents significant new capability.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: No architectural changes required. Implementation may need robust configuration interfaces for client-side setup.

Add a logging subsystem with immutable storage (e.g., blockchain or write-once databases) integrated into 'Governance Manager' for decisions and 'FL Manager'/'Communicator' for training contributions. Ensure 7-year retention and compliance access controls.
issues_identified: Potential performance impact from logging overhead
Need for data lifecycle management for retention
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Use external audit services with APIs, but this may reduce traceability integration.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: No architectural changes required. Develop a user-friendly API/UI layer that calls existing components.

Modify 'Governance Manager' to support pluggable authentication providers via standardized interfaces (e.g., OAuth 2.0). Update 'Communicator' to handle token-based authentication without altering core RPC/HTTP2 protocols.
issues_identified: Potential security gaps if integration is misconfigured
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Use a federation gateway that translates between custom providers and a standard internal auth protocol.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Enhance 'FL Client Model Deployer' with CI/CD integration hooks (e.g., webhooks, APIs) and automation triggers. Improve 'Communicator' reliability for timely model delivery. Add monitoring in 'FL Manager' for deployment status.
issues_identified: Variability in client CI/CD tools may require custom adapters
One-hour SLA may be challenging over unreliable networks
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Allow clients to poll for models and self-deploy, but this reduces automation.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Extend 'FL Manager' with simulation capabilities (sandbox environment, synthetic data generators). Add a new 'Simulation Manager' component. Update 'Governance Manager' for simulation policy management.
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Use offline analysis tools external to FL-APU to estimate data impact, but this lacks integration.

scenario_interaction_weights.rationale: C-01 is the most frequently impacted component (5 scenarios), spanning high-risk changes like simulation (S-06, high effort/risk) and logging (S-02, widespread medium risk). Neglecting it risks compliance failures (S-02), security gaps (S-04), and inability to simulate FL behavior (S-06), affecting all stakeholder roles due to systemic reliability and regulatory exposure.

Involved in 4 scenarios including two high-impact ones: S-06 (simulation, high risk/effort) and S-02 (logging, medium risk). Also supports CI/CD automation (S-05) affecting deployment reliability. Failure to address could lead to poor orchestration, missed SLAs, and lack of auditability, directly impacting developers and administrators, with indirect effects on end-users through model delivery delays.

Affected by 4 scenarios, primarily involving authentication (S-04) and logging (S-02), both medium effort/risk. Also part of CI/CD enhancements (S-05). While changes are localized, ignoring it could compromise security integration and compliance logging, increasing administrative overhead and potential access vulnerabilities, indirectly affecting end-user trust.

Only impacted by 2 low-risk, low-effort scenarios (S-01, S-03) requiring minimal changes like configuration interfaces or API/UI layers. Neglect poses minimal business risk—mainly minor usability or setup inefficiencies for developers and end-users, with no critical operational or compliance consequences.

architectural_strengths: Strength 1: The architecture's core components (C-01, C-02, C-03, C-04) are well-defined and modular, enabling direct support for user-facing scenarios with minimal changes. For S-01 and S-03, no architectural changes are required; only configuration interfaces or API/UI layers are needed, indicating strong foundational design for basic federated learning operations and user interactions.

Strength 2: The architecture demonstrates good separation of concerns, allowing localized modifications for specific enhancements. For S-04 (pluggable authentication) and S-05 (CI/CD integration), changes are localized to specific components (C-01, C-04, C-03, C-05) without widespread impact, showing that components like 'Governance Manager' and 'Communicator' have clear interfaces that support incremental improvements.
architectural_weaknesses: Weakness 1: The architecture lacks built-in support for cross-cutting concerns like auditing and simulation, requiring widespread changes. S-02 (logging) demands integration across multiple components (C-01, C-03, C-04) with immutable storage, exposing gaps in compliance and traceability features. Similarly, S-06 (simulation) requires a new 'Simulation Manager' and extensions to C-03 and C-01, indicating high complexity and resource overhead for advanced capabilities.

Weakness 2: Component C-01 is a critical hotspot with high coupling across five scenarios (S-01, S-02, S-03, S-04, S-06), making it a single point of failure for modifiability. Its involvement in high-risk changes (e.g., simulation, logging) suggests it may be overburdened, increasing the risk of regression and slowing evolution for business goals like traceability and collaboration.
overall_results.modifiability_assessment: The architecture supports low-to-medium effort changes well through modular components, but struggles with high-effort, widespread modifications for cross-cutting features, indicating moderate evolvability with risks around core hotspots like C-01.

recommendations.recommendation: Refactor C-01 ('Governance Manager') to decouple cross-cutting concerns by introducing a dedicated 'Audit & Logging Service' and a 'Simulation Service' as separate microservices or modules, integrating them via well-defined APIs.
recommendations.rationale: This reduces coupling in C-01, isolates compliance and simulation logic, and improves modifiability for S-02 and S-06 by allowing independent updates without impacting core governance functions.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement a standardized plugin framework for authentication (e.g., OAuth 2.0 adapters) in C-04 ('Communicator') and C-01, ensuring security and testing automation to address S-04 issues.
recommendations.rationale: Enhances flexibility for diverse authentication providers, reduces integration risks, and supports business goals of combining governance with authentication in cross-silo applications.

recommendations.recommendation: Add monitoring and resilience patterns to C-03 ('FL Manager') and C-04 to support CI/CD automation (S-05), including retry mechanisms and status dashboards for deployment SLAs.
recommendations.rationale: Improves reliability for model delivery and automation, addressing network variability and enhancing modifiability for future DevOps integrations.

recommendations.recommendation: Develop comprehensive configuration management for C-02 and other components, leveraging existing APIs to streamline setup for S-01 and S-03 as a quick win.
recommendations.rationale: Reduces usability friction for developers and end-users with minimal effort, supporting ease of use in real-world applications.
